topic: A person finds out their biography was written without their knowledge
raw_output:
[story 1]

The crisp, cream-colored paper felt like a physical insult under my fingertips. I stared at the meticulously crafted narrative, the words detailing my life—my triumphs, my supposed struggles—all spun by someone else’s hand. A slow, cold burn started low in my stomach and spread outward, tightening my chest until breathing became a deliberate, shallow effort. I crumpled the document, the sound sharp in the quiet study. I stood up, pacing the length of the room, my jaw clenched so tightly my molars ached. Every corner of the room seemed to hold the ghost of the author, the unseen manipulator who had decided my existence was a draft to be edited. I needed to find them. I needed to dismantle the illusion they had so carefully constructed around me.

[story 2]

When the lawyer handed over the final draft of my life story, the air left my lungs in a rush. It wasn't just inaccurate; it was a deliberate, calculated misrepresentation. A deep, simmering resentment coiled in my gut, a hot, unpleasant energy that made my hands tremble slightly as I gripped the file. I looked at the signature line, the name that belonged to the scribe, and a sharp, brittle laugh escaped my throat. It was a sound devoid of humor, purely corrosive. I didn't bother thanking the lawyer. Instead, I walked out, the polished marble floors echoing my agitated stride. I wanted to find the person responsible, to confront them with the sheer, infuriating audacity of their deception.

[story 3]

I traced the elegant script with a shaking finger, the ink seeming to mock me. Every sentence was a carefully placed stone in a wall built to confine me. A profound sense of injustice washed over me, heavy and suffocating. My knuckles were white where I held the paper, the tension radiating up my arms. I didn't cry; tears felt too weak for the magnitude of the offense. Instead, I smoothed the paper flat on the desk, a deliberate, almost aggressive action, before tearing a corner clean off. The resulting ragged edge felt satisfyingly real. I wanted to burn the whole thing, to watch the fabricated history turn to ash, a silent vow forming in the hollow of my throat.
